evidence: QueryExecutor interface has a single production implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor) — documented in Javadoc as 'primarily a testability seam rather than an abstraction boundary'; this is honest and appropriate self-labelling

SemanticFieldExplorer acts as a coordination facade: owns single-seed algorithm directly, delegates multi-seed to MultiSeedExplorer and comparison to CollocateProfileComparator — explicitly documented design rationale

GrammarConfigSerializer extracted from GrammarConfig/RelationConfig so config objects remain pure data carriers — appropriate extraction that prevents JSON-library coupling from leaking into the model layer
ExploreResponseAssembler owns all model-to-presentation translation for exploration endpoints — single, coherent responsibility
HttpApiUtils co-locates response-writing and request-parsing; Javadoc explicitly argues why splitting would add navigational friction without architectural benefit — defensible
RelationPatternBuilder is a pure static utility extracted from RelationConfig record so the record stays data-only — appropriate
RelationUtils is a pure static utility for alias resolution — appropriate scope
SingleSeedExplorationOptions.toBaseOptions() is a pure pass-through: return base; — minimal value, kept as a named projection but adds no behavior or policy
ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions options-object pattern pays for itself via binary-compatible extension points and named parameters at call sites
ComparisonResult.fullyShared(), partiallyShared(), specific(), specificTo() are stream-filter views on allAdjectives() — duplicating category logic already in AdjectiveProfile.sharingCategory()

strengths: QueryExecutor interface is honest about being a testability seam — does not pretend to be a port with multiple implementations
GrammarConfigSerializer/RelationPatternBuilder/RelationUtils are all correctly extracted as pure static utilities with no artificial object overhead
SemanticFieldExplorer facade is explicitly documented with its coordination role and stability contract
ExploreResponseAssembler is a genuine separation of concerns — it owns the model-to-JSON translation and nothing else
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: Abstractions in this codebase generally earn their keep; the main fitness issue is ComparisonResult accumulating stream-filter views that duplicate logic already in AdjectiveProfile, and SingleSeedExplorationOptions.toBaseOptions() being a pure return pass-through that adds a name without adding behavior.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: Most abstractions in the codebase are appropriately scoped and earn their indirection through genuine leverage. The score is in the low 80s because a small number of pass-throughs and duplicated filter views add surface without value. No delegation-heavy classes or facade-only re-export modules were found.

issues.summary: ComparisonResult.fullyShared/partiallyShared/specific duplicate filtering logic already encoded in AdjectiveProfile.sharingCategory()
evidence: ComparisonResult.fullyShared(): filters adjectives where presentInCount == nouns.size() — same condition as AdjectiveProfile.isFullyShared()
ComparisonResult.partiallyShared(): filters where presentInCount >= 2 && presentInCount < nouns.size() — same condition as AdjectiveProfile.isPartiallyShared()
AdjectiveProfile already encodes the sharing category via sharingCategory() which returns a SharingCategory enum
The filter methods in ComparisonResult are only used externally in tests or assembler code; ExploreResponseAssembler currently uses allAdjectives() and then calls adj.sharingCategory()
issues.suggestion: Remove fullyShared(), partiallyShared(), specific(), and specificTo() from ComparisonResult. Callers that need filtered subsets should filter allAdjectives() by adj.sharingCategory(). Keep summaryCounts() as it is a genuinely efficient single-pass count.

issues.summary: SingleSeedExplorationOptions.toBaseOptions() is a pure return pass-through with no added behavior or policy
evidence: public ExplorationOptions toBaseOptions() { return base; } — identical to accessing the base field directly
The recent change note says '3 pass-through delegation methods removed; callers use opts.base() directly' — toBaseOptions() appears to be a fourth that was retained
issues.suggestion: Remove toBaseOptions() and update callers to use opts.base() directly, consistent with the refactoring direction already taken for the other three delegation methods.

root_causes: ComparisonResult grew filter views incrementally as callers requested convenience; toBaseOptions() survived the recent delegation-method cleanup
